speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 20th of November, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Miriam Freedman. This is tape number three. You were just talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, it's tape number four. You were just talking about your miraculous escape from being caught by the German. If you'd like to continue with that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when Pernik came back, Pernik came back a few hours later and told us that the German was convinced there aren't Jews in this place and the miraculous escape of not entering into our apartment, he himself was quite surprised what happened. We were tremendously relieved, obviously, but we never knew what was next going to happen, obviously. It was always touch and go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On a number of occasions, there was-- the neighbors were suspicious. Not the next door, but the other neighbors. So we carried on staying there for, uh-- till the end of the war. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still spending a lot of time in this hole in the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- yes. We used to go down. Every time when some news came about, that this was only the biggest incidence of them suspecting us. But there were other incidents of suspecting Jews. But then we found out there was another-- one or two other people hiding in the mill, by somebody else. That, we found out after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the news came, of course, that gradually that the Russian soldiers are getting nearer to our area. And we taken down again to the cellar. Again-- this time, we were taken down to the cellar for a different reason. Because there was a rumor that the Russian army are approaching, and the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] army are getting panicky. They really did not know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were put down in the cellar, so that-- just to save ourselves from the shooting. Now, at this time, there were plane coming-- bombing. The army was coming now. You know, it was a different kind of problem. It wasn't now about ourselves only, but it was a whole town-- a whole country. Did you know which army was approaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we were told by news from other people that there is a Russian army getting nearer. They're defeating the German army. And so they're moving nearer and nearer. And we had to now go to the cellar for different reason. Instead of us Jews, also where other people for physical safety. Upstairs, this was a block of flats. It was quite obvious to see, a small town. And the planes were quite low. We could hear them pretty loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose army or whose air force did the planes come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, did other people in that apartment block know that you had-- were Jewish? Did they see you down in the cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did not. We still were in the hole. Because we-- The people who were living in the building would come down only for a short period when there were bombing going. Or some shooting was coming. But they wouldn't stay there to carry on with the normal daily work. But we stayed behind. We couldn't go up and down. They just could not take us, physically-- move us around. We stayed there, now, for quite a long time. I can't tell you how much longer it was, but it was, like-- even Mr. Pernik did not know how near the army is and what exactly is happening. But then you could see the Germans are running away and leaving the town. So that was the first step, to know that something is changed. But you must remember that a lot of Slovak cooperated with the-- with the Germans. They managed to get quite substantial people. So you had to be very careful. Anyway, this was lasting a few weeks, this battle, backwards and forward. So we must have been most of the time in the cellar or in the basement. And then, uh, the Russians crossed the river. And the first building, it was ours. Because it was the most prestigious building. We didn't know what's going on. And this I remember very clearly. We were sitting there. A Russian officer comes down. Actually, he looked terribly nice, aesthetically, and clean. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he just saw the wretched people sitting here. He couldn't make out what it was. Because, you see, this was a completely different army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this you that he found?

speaker: interviewee
text: Us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but, you see, he was taken down by Pernik. Pernik had tried to explain, but they did not know. You see, the Russian army, who was fighting the front, the battlefield, did not know about anything like that. They did not probably even could read and write, most of them. They couldn't understand what's going on with the Jews were persecuted, and they had to go in hiding, and-- the Final Solution. They did not know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to enlighten this person. The problem was that we must not speak German. You see, we all could speak German. It would have come advantage if we had to live under the German occupation. But, no, the Russian knew that anybody speaks German must be the enemy. So we had to tell this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Apparently it's back. We're rolling again.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've just had a little technical hitch, and now we're rolling again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russian officer came down to the basement and pat me. I remember he gave me, I think, the first sweet I had for possibly a year. I treasured it tremendously. And he was telling us what's going on outside. He explained, via interpreter-- I think our caretaker spoke Russian-- what's going on. That the army's coming over, and they haven't seen a woman for years. And all these women were very beautiful, attractive women in their 30s, 40s. There were probably some a bit older, but-- And now a new hiding started. We had to hide away from the Russians. This was quite a surprise. I couldn't wait to be liberated. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so the women were hiding for some time in the cellar, from the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, when he came, was that your moment of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume it was our liberation. This was the moment that they came and took over the occupation. I don't know whether it was a general liberation of all the war, but liberation of our town-- occupied by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you all came out of this hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. We couldn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at the point that the Russian arrived, you came out of the hiding place, to speak to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The point that we were in a cellar with the Russian officer. We did not actually leave the cellar yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you come out of the hole in the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yes. Yes, yes, yes. And we were talking to this Russian man. And he just warned via Pernik that the women have to be very careful, because we have got here an army coming who haven't seen a woman for years-- or for a long period. And it is a very, very ignorant and primitive army. Also, we're delighted, because we're going to be liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the River Nitra, what is about 30 feet, I can swim it quite easily. There's nothing to it. They thought they reached River Volga. So uninformed were the Russian army. Can you imagine, coming from Russia all the way  to Czechoslovakia, not realizing that where they were physically, actually? And they really thought they were still in Russia. So this was quite a surprise to us, because the German army, on the other hand, were very, very smartly dressed and a very educated lot. And here we found these soldiers arriving. We had to be very careful. But gradually, we moved out, eventually, after some time, to an apartment-- what was also vacant. And my uncle-- we moved up there. Still all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still all together-- for a little while.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lady who saved you, Helenka. Was she related to the man, the caretaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The caretaker was married to Maria. They had a daughter, Duli. Helenka was the fiancee of William Gavalovic. Her name was Helena Galova. She was working together with William in the mill, as a secretary, and she became his wife, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was also involved with your rescue.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very much involved with our rescue. She kept our morale up. Helenka and William, they used to come every single day after work to spend all the free time with us-- to humor us, to tell us the news, give us a little occupation. And they spent hours. And, of course, we were very, very fond of her. So she was very, very young. But she become like one of our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you moved to this new apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved upstairs. And I would like to tell you now the incident that happened previously was that Pernik said-- told me one day, when we were in hiding, around Christmas, I'm going to bring you a present. And I was looking forward so much to the present. He brought me his daughter, Duli, who was my age. And Duli, you must visualize it-- she was a kid of eight or nine year old, who was put in a big secret, to know there are Jews or people hiding, and a little girl of her own age. As you can visualize, as any child would do, when they go back to school, would say, I know something-- you don't know something. But somehow they managed to put that it is sort of big secret. And Duli and I had very little to say first time that she came to visit me. I was so excited, like getting a real gift, to be with another child, that all I was saying to her that, when the liberation will come, I'm going to race you. And I will walk and run that you will never catch me. But-- and that was something so terribly important for me, to be able to walk and to be out in nature and to be able to see fresh air and birds and flowers and trees. And when actually, physically this happened, the war was over, and Duli said, well, now you have to go race me, a few days after the liberation. And, of course, my leg was so swollen, because the circulation stopped-- we didn't do any movement-- that I hardly can walk for about a month. This was a very important incident for me to remind you that things like that were happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue to live in that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we-- right a few days after, went back to our old house in Podzamska, August the 29th or 28th-- went to look for our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] belonging, and of course, finding out what's happened to our father.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still in Nitra.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is still in Nitra. So we went back out of this-- when we become slightly quieter, after about a week or two, when we really felt we could get out. It was very, very dangerous to go out because practically every woman, young and old, was raped, in the whole town. Nine months afterwards, it was an incredible city. Nearly everybody was pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to that-- to our apartment, just to see that the building was bombed. And, strangely enough, it was not looted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was bombed. The neighbors who-- actually, they must have been worried. So we got there, and we found an apartment across the road, just opposite. And there we had some help to remove some of the furniture, whatever there was found able to use. We moved it to the across-the-road apartment. Also a basic one, but a better one than the previous one. Ah, see, that must have been 29, and we must have been 28 before. And there we moved after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now the whole thing started-- rehabilitation. And we had to start really-- gra-- what actually happened. We didn't really know what was happening. We know it was madness, but we really didn't understand exactly what's happening in Germany, or in Europe, or Poland. We didn't know nothing about it. We only knew what I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So an organization called UNRRA-- I think-- United Nation organization for rescue, of its kind. And they opened an office and in that Vatican area, and we were going to look for our family. That was our main goal, to find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gradually, we found out that it was a tremendous war, and the Jews disappeared-- Jews didn't come back. So, all of sudden, we could see hardly anybody-- I think 80 people out of hundreds of hundreds of hundreds of people in Bratislava and Nitra who actually survived. I think the whole city-- in Nitra, 80 people survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we tried to find out, obviously, what happened to my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The thing was, the last time I saw him-- when I looked out the window, when he was across the road-- he went away, back, obviously not knowing what to do. During the day-- This is the information that we received from the neighbors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who said that, obviously, why they didn't help him-- sorry-- why they didn't help him to be saved or rescued, that I would not know. But the thing is we found out after the war that during the day, he went out in the fields [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and was hiding wherever he could. Nighttime, he was coming back to the cottage. And all he got was some bread and coffee. And then he was, of course, sleeping overnight. The next day was going out again. And this proceeding was lasting for a long time, because he just probably was praying, hoping for some kind of help from somebody what didn't come. Eventually-- that must be the second month, something, during that-- the summer was still on, but I think, probably some time later, he must have-- somebody informed about him. And some Germans or the Hlinka army came in, and they must have been tipped off. They came in. And he probably must have seen them coming-- spilled the coffee out, but the kettle apparently was still warm. So they realized he couldn't got very far. They tried looking for it-- for him. And we had very large eiderdown. Apparently, he was hiding near the mattress, under the mattress, but they found him. And they just tied him up like a pig and just-- by foot, And they took him out to the car or van, whatever was outside. And he was taken down to one of the transports. Was taken to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a story we got from somebody who met him on the way was, he was still a gentleman, what he was. A pregnant woman was put on the transport, and he gave her a seat. And, of course, he was travelling for days, now standing. He found a little corner to sit on a train, but a woman came in with a-- pregnant woman, and he stood up and gave her the place to sit down. So the whole journey, he was standing. That kind of man, he was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When he got into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Auschwitz-- I think it was Auschwitz-- he met my brother, I was told. My brother was taken by the-- a prisoner of war by the Russians. Uh-- by the German, but that Russian soldier. Sorry. He was in a-- in a group of Russian prisoners. And, of course, they also survived. I'm very envious of this lot, because they had to treat the Russian prisoners slightly better, because they may need an exchange, or they didn't know that the war is going to develop. Apparently, he met my father. And, on one or two occasion, marching around, and threw him some bread over the fence, the electric fence. This is what we were told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people who-- there was an organization that-- people coming back. Some people survived, and some people met them on their journey. We don't know-- I don't know the person itself, but they told our family. And my father then was moved to the Austrian-Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] border, to a concentration camp called Ebensee. There he worked in a cement factory. And, uh-- tortured, and died. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that's what we got. I went back to Ebensee to-- to put a stone up for him, years later, strange enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to your brother? Well, that-- that is a horrendous story. Horrendous story. Because he survived three days before the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because the Russians-- they did not know. The Germans, they may need them to exchange the Russian army. And the guard was panicky, and he shot them all. Where was he, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Auschwitz. But he was a strong man. He was quite-- he was 18, he was quite a healthy young-- young man. He could have been alive. Also could have my father been alive. But he was, till the last two or three days. Then apparently, the guard, who was trying run away, did not know what to do. Everybody's gone, he didn't know what to do with the prisoners. So he shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sister who also went away with your brother, originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she went to Hungary. I didn't tell you about my brother, but, of course, my brother tried to come back. The way he was caught-- he was in Hungary, then things become bad in Hungary, and he wanted to come back to Slovakia. And on the border, he was caught with my sister, and he was taken away. Not quite sure was my sister together on his own. But my sister was in Hungary with my family, and then she was caught and gone on the Eichmann march, where she perished on the-- she was only 15 or so. She was very young. But she was very delicate. She was a very, very unusual young woman. She was a pianist and a singer. Very, very gifted. She was probably gifted-- the most gifted of all of us. Very bright girl. And she was very delicate, as well. And she died from the march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you survive with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Gertie, my mother, and we-- we survived that, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Cellar. Edith, who was taken to Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was caught very quickly after the second day, when we separated. You remember, the beginning, we separated. We went to look for our father, and she was looking to find out information-- what is going on. And then we got lost, and she probably did not know where we were. And she went in hiding in a haystack. It was summer season. And she was caught very quickly, I think within 24 hours. She had to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was taken to Auschwitz. But she herself happened to be-- probably the only one who looked much more older than she was. Well, how old could she have been? She was five years-- she must have been about 14 or 15. I don't exactly know. Probably less.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was developed. And when she was taken to Auschwitz, they were selecting people to go to the right and people going to the left. And she was very intuitive or very aware. When she looked on the left, they all looked a bit older people, with glasses and-- she managed to slip, or somebody gave her a tip. It looks like those that are going to the right side look younger and healthier. I mean, you did not know what it meant, anyway. Could be that they went to [INAUDIBLE]. You did not know they're going to go to gas chamber. But she went to escape to the other side. And she was in a labor camp. And, unfortunately, Mengele did experiment on her. And she was very fat, because they put all kind of hormones. They needed a group of people, and the Red Cross used to come from time to time to examine the camps, to see how they treat the prisoners. Obviously, if you don't look thin, obviously, you got food. And so that was partly that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of experiments. She's still suffering from it, I think, today. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: She survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived. And she spoke German. And she was-- in her camp, quite a lot of people-- I think Yugoslavian and Serb and from different nationality. And so they used her as the interpreter. That came to advantage sometime. She survived, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in Israel, in a kibbutz, Nir David, with her family. Three children. 14 grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living when you found out all this information about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- I told you-- we still lived in the same street-- Podzamska [INAUDIBLE]-- but we went across the road. And I went to school. I went to school. I didn't go to a Jewish school, I went to a state school run by nuns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when my-- Edith came of-- came back. That's another long story I'll tell you, how my sister came back. At that time-- sorry, I beg your pardon. At that time, we didn't know. At that time we moved to-- to my auntie. We had another auntie who came back, who had a big house in Nitra. And we moved to her place for a while. [INAUDIBLE] and Hugo, they survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we moved there. And my mother used to go every day or afternoon, possibly, to find out if any member of our family survived, because that office was actually getting information from other offices.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were told that Edith is alive. [INAUDIBLE] and her father perished. We were told quite early about that. And-- but we did not know where she was and when she's going to come back and how she's going to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she came back. And that was also a horrendous experience for me. She was dressed in a German military coat, because whatever she could find to wear. She was very shaven still. And her hair started to grow a little. And when she arrived to our town and to our flat-- house-- where still is our auntie, she knocked on the door. And I got such a shock, when I saw she was a German soldier, and slammed the door on her. And then she said, 'Edith, Edith, Edith.' We opened the door, and it was a very, very special little [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother cope, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was always an introvert person, but I think she become more introverted now. Her husband was killed, and two of her children. Then I remember her brother, [INAUDIBLE]. And many other member of my late father-- my father's family, most of them perished. They were a very large family. We coped. We went to the other place. We were waiting to hear any news. And then, of course, we got news that my sister in Palestine was still-- tried to contact us. And she really tried very hard to get us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got letters from her, and she tried to get us a visa or permit to go to Palestine, at that time. So there was not much point to stay anymore in Slovakia. Our possession, everything-- our home was destroyed. My father was not alive. And so my mother made a decision to go with us to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- meantime, I went to school, and I enjoyed um-- it's very strange what happened after the war. Immediately, different organizations opened youth movements in the Vatican area. And there was Bnei Akiva and Shomer Hatzair and Hamaccabi Hatzair and all the movement-- they're sending people and trying to induce Zionistic ideas into us and, uh-- It was quite interesting to see-- something to do and something to look forward-- something to hope. And I used to go there nearly every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11? I think I was 11-- about. And I went to a non-Jewish school now and had to learn Russian. And, uh-- proper school, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to adapt to this new life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the hardest bit to describe to anybody-- describe anybody that you have been in running around from one town to another town, to be in hiding, now you're liberated, now you find out that half of your family are dead, and you're a child. And, uh, I kept always saying, when is my father coming back? When am I going to see my sister, my brother. It's very difficult to explain. A new consciousness. Kind of another person. Something is sort of cut off-- part of your life. But somehow you can smile, and you can laugh, and you can play games. But there's a new consciousness came about. And I went to school, and I did my normal thing, and I had my girlfriend, and we used to go here and there. And I'm visiting my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: My auntie stayed in the same block of flats. She got a flat-- the ground-floor flat, she got, where we used to hide in one room. Next door was a larger one. We used to go to see our auntie. And now we have to decide how we're going to build our new life. What's going to happen now? We are at the end of the next tape.
